items.q: What was the theory of the four humours?
items.correct: Disease was caused by imbalance of blood, phlegm, yellow bile and black bile; treatment aimed to rebalance them via bloodletting, purging or diet. It dominated for nearly 2,000 years.
items.misconception: Medieval doctors knew about germs but used humours because they had no medicine.
items.random_wrong: The four humours were invented by Pasteur to explain bacteria.
items.why: Humoral theory was a coherent system from Hippocrates and Galen, not ignorance. Students retroject germ theory backwards, missing why educated people believed it for so long.

items.q: Why did Henry VIII break with Rome?
items.correct: Primarily for an annulment from Catherine of Aragon, but also to seize Church wealth and assert royal supremacy — not for Protestant theological reasons.
items.misconception: Because he wanted England to become Protestant.
items.random_wrong: Because the Pope excommunicated him for marrying Anne Boleyn.
items.why: Henry remained doctrinally Catholic to his death. The break was political and dynastic; Protestantism only took hold in England under Edward VI and Elizabeth I.

items.q: Why were Jews expelled from England in 1290?
items.correct: Edward I expelled Jews after a century of restrictions and taxation had impoverished the community; antisemitism, debts owed to Jews, and political pressure all contributed.
items.misconception: Jews left England voluntarily because they were no longer welcome.
items.random_wrong: Jews were expelled because they refused to fight in the Crusades.
items.why: Expulsion was a royal edict, not voluntary. Students sometimes assume migrations are always chosen; medieval Jewish history is a story of state coercion.

items.q: In which month and year did the Cuban Missile Crisis take place?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: October 1962. “April 1961” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What did Kennedy call the naval blockade of Cuba to avoid legal complications?
items.why: It's Quarantine. “Embargo” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately how many days did the 'Thirteen Days' of the crisis last?
items.why: Exact answer: 13. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
